feedback.text: Trade of digital goods needs to be more regulated. Currently many companies are leasing licenses to their software to consumers under the guise of selling. This is especially prevalent in video games market, where a lot of products consumers purchased lost support and in consequence consumers completely lost access to it. One such example is game called The Crew, where publisher of the game Ubisoft has decided to shutdown servers and consequently prevented consumers from accessing it in any way, where the product was supposedly sold and not leased. There is already citizen initiative called Stop Killing Games, and gaming publisher lobby tries to maliciously misrepresent what the initiative stands for. They claim that providing support for older games would be very costly, where the initiative asks that publishers will leave games in reasonably working state such that consumers who purchased them can still enjoy them after the support reached its end of life, even if that would mean that some mechanics will become unavailable. And the initiative calls for this to only apply to video games that could be enjoyed solo, not for so called live service games where many consumers are already aware they will lose access to the game once it will reach its end of life, as there is no possibility to leave such game in reasonably playable state, or this would be very costly for the company who created the game. Also many developers working in those companies already came forward saying that when the game is developed, a form of private servers or offline mode is implemented for testing. Their next claim is that they could not do it for the fear of consumer safety, as even if they would leave the game active consumers would be exposed to malicious actors such as hackers or cheaters. Problem with that statement is that many currently still supported games are riddled witch cheaters and hackers, who are sometimes able to affect accounts of other consumers and e.g. delete items those consumers bought within game using micro transactions. The same companies do practically nothing to mitigate this problem, and their only solution is to implement even more aggressive anti-cheat solutions which are put in the consumers operating system kernel, which not only allows those companies to access consumers private data without their knowledge, but also makes their systems more vulnerable to malicious attacks if such software would have become compromised. And it even does not really work as all of the games are using it still have cheaters, so this level of access on consumers devices is not even justified. The biggest offender here is again Ubisoft but as well as Take2Interactive with their flagship product Grand Theft Auto 5 Online which is plagued with hackers and cheaters, but the company is not doing anything about this. Ubisoft is also guilty of predatory and anti consumer practices, such as deleting inactive accounts which had purchased products linked to them. I was one such a person, where account which was registered under email address maciej.zubkowski@gmail.com was deleted, where I have not received any prior communication regarding the possibility of this happening. The only information I later found out was in their terms of use, which fair enough however the practice itself of deleting digital purchases of consumer simply because they have not logged in for some time and not even having the curtesy to inform said consumer that if they will not log in to their account they will loose it screams of malicious practices. When it comes to micro transactions, companies should have the right to implement them and consumers should vote with their wallet if they approve of such practices. However some types of micro transactions and game mechanics build around them are designed to be highly addictive and closely resemble gambling. This is especially prevalent in mobile gaming market where the largest audience are children.
